coredata.dc:title: Scalability of sulfur-functionalized carbon quantum dots from peanut shells: A sustainable sensor of high colorimetric heavy metal detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Javeria, Huma
Abbas, Muhammad Qamer
Chen, Shu-Huan
Keshat, Basem E.
Du, Zhenxia
coredata.dc:description: The presence of heavy metals in food and drinking water is one of the most significant risks to people's well-being. Therefore, it is important to establish highly sensitive, selective, and inexpensive procedures for determining heavy metals. A novel strategy for fabricating heavy metal sensors that is both effective and environmentally friendly has been developed in this research. Therefore, this study aims to develop, scalable, and characterize sulfur-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that are prepared from peanut shells to detect heavy metals. Citric acid and peanut shells (PSCA) are used as a source of carbon quantum dots and modified with sulfur powder (S@PSCA) and sodium sulfate (SO4@PSCA) to enhance their fluorescence and color-changing properties, which are characterized by several techniques showing that the addition of sulfur compounds have changed the overall properties of PSCA CQDs. The synthesized S@PSCA CQDs and SO4@PSCA CQDs were tested for colorimetric sensing of heavy metals in the concentration range of 5–85 nM. S@PSCA detected Pb2 + (white precipitates) and Ag+ (yellow color) with the LOD 3.83 nM and 9 µM. NaS@PSCA CQDs detected Ag+ (milky white color) with the LOD 8.2 nM. Novel CQD offers potential advantages, such as low cost, eco-friendliness, and high performance. As a result, this research opens up new opportunities for the rapid and visual detection of heavy metals in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peanut shells
Waste recycling
Colorimetric detection
Quantum dots, Heavy metal sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image particle size histogram of S@PSCA CQD (Inset), (b) TEM image and particle size histogram of SO4@PSCA CQD(Inset), and (c, d) FTIR spectra and XRD patterns of PSCA, SO4@PSCA, and S@PSCA CQDs, respectively.
(a-d) C1s scan, O1s scan, S2p scan and N1s scan of S@PSCA CQD (e-h) C1s scan, O1s scan, S2p scan and N1s scan of SO4@PSCA CQD.

(a) Maximum Ex & Em of S@PSCA, (b) Concentration optimization of S@PSCA, (c) Excitation shift of S@PSCA, (d) Maximum Ex & Em of SO4@PSCA, (e) Concentration optimization of SO4@PSCA, and (e) Excitation shift of SO4@PSCA.

(a) Combined absorbance spectra of PSCA, S@PSCA, and SO4@PSCA, (b) Decay time of PSCA, S@PSCA, and SO4@PSCA, (c) Fluorescent pH stability of S@PSCA (d) UV–visible pH stability of S@PSCA, (e) Fluorescent pH stability of SO4@PSCA, and (f) UV–visible pH stability of SO4@PSCA.
(a) Metal selectivity of S@PSCA and (b) Metal selectivity of SO4@PSCA.

(a) Effect of increasing concentration of Pb2+ with S@PSCA, (b) Limit of detection calculation of Pb2+ with S@PSCA, (c) Effect of increasing concentration of Ag+ with S@PSCA, (d) Limit of detection calculation of Ag+ with S@PSCA, (e) Effect of increasing concentration of Ag+ with SO4@PSCA, and (f) Limit of detection calculation of Ag+ with SO4@PSCA.
Sulfur-functionalized PSCA-derived CQDs (S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA). with enhanced fluorescence are synthesized for colorimetric detection of Ag+ and Pb2+ ions.
Comparison of S-modified PSCA CQDs’ quantum yield with previously reported research.
LOD comparison of Pb2+ and Ag+ ions between SO4@PSCA, S@PSCA and reported literature.

row.entry: Lignin-mediated green synthesis of functionalized gold nanoparticles via pulsed laser technique for selective colorimetric detection of lead ions in aqueous media
Colorimetric determination of lead using gold nanoparticles
Preparation of gold nanoparticles using pyridine-formaldehyde as a reducing agent and its application in high-sensitivity colorimetric detection of Pb2+

Selective colorimetric detection of Pb (II) ions by using green synthesized gold nanoparticles with orange peel extract
Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions
Hydrothermal synthesis of B, S, and N-modified carbon quantum dots for colorimetric sensing of heavy metal ions
Chiral CQD-based PL and CD sensors for highly sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample analysis results of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs.

head.ce:title: Scalability of sulfur-functionalized carbon quantum dots from peanut shells: A sustainable sensor of high colorimetric heavy metal detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Tanta University, Tanta, El Gharbia 31527, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Tanta University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Tanta University, Tanta, El Gharbia 31527, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of heavy metals in food and drinking water is one of the most significant risks to people's well-being. Therefore, it is important to establish highly sensitive, selective, and inexpensive procedures for determining heavy metals. A novel strategy for fabricating heavy metal sensors that is both effective and environmentally friendly has been developed in this research. Therefore, this study aims to develop, scalable, and characterize sulfur-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that are prepared from peanut shells to detect heavy metals. Citric acid and peanut shells (PSCA) are used as a source of carbon quantum dots and modified with sulfur powder (S@PSCA) and sodium sulfate (SO4@PSCA) to enhance their fluorescence and color-changing properties, which are characterized by several techniques showing that the addition of sulfur compounds have changed the overall properties of PSCA CQDs. The synthesized S@PSCA CQDs and SO4@PSCA CQDs were tested for colorimetric sensing of heavy metals in the concentration range of 5–85 nM. S@PSCA detected Pb2 + (white precipitates) and Ag+ (yellow color) with the LOD 3.83 nM and 9 µM. NaS@PSCA CQDs detected Ag+ (milky white color) with the LOD 8.2 nM. Novel CQD offers potential advantages, such as low cost, eco-friendliness, and high performance. As a result, this research opens up new opportunities for the rapid and visual detection of heavy metals in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The contamination of the environment by heavy metals is a global concern due to its harmful consequences on human health [1]. Silver (Ag) metal has many properties that are particularly useful for antibacterial applications, medicine, and cosmetics [2]. However, the Ag metal's concentration can be toxic to humans if the amount exceeds a certain limit. In addition, when the body is rich in Ag metal, enzymes that have sulfhydryl groups become denatured [3]. Further, depending on the amount of Ag metal, it may lead to argentosis [4], abdominal pain [5], dermatitis [6], cell membrane disruption [7], edema of organs [8], and even death. Therefore, silver ion detection and environmental monitoring are of great importance. Lead (Pb) is one of the heavy metals that has some uses in industries, and even at low concentrations, it can cause learning disabilities, behavioral disorders, and low IQ [9]. In high exposure, it may result in lead-induced seizures [10], lead encephalopathy [11], and the worst case, death. Therefore, to resolve these problems, the major concern is to design a rapid, accurate, easy, and cheap sensor for the selective determination of the lead and silver ions in pure samples.

In recent years, there has been a growing focus on detecting heavy metals and using quantum dots in sensing devices[12]. Colorimetric sensors are frequently used to detect heavy metals due to their ease of use, simplicity, and rapid detection [13]. Despite a lot of research, it has been challenging to use carbon quantum dots prepared from green materials to fabricate colorimetric sensors for heavy metals due to their inconsistent properties, difficult surface functionalization, and potential issues with stability, sensitivity, and selectivity[14]. These challenges are associated with the inherent variabilities of green materials. Yuyuan has prepared CQDS from coffee beans and used them for the colorimetric detection of ascorbic acid [15]. Chunfeng Liang has prepared Iron-modified CQDs with Passiflora edulis and used them for the colorimetric detection of uric acid [16]. Prema and his co-workers have prepared nanoparticles from green tea extract and used them for the colorimetric detection of mercury [17]. None of these researchers has used these green materials for colorimetric detection of lead and silver. Although years of research have been conducted on green materials for the preparation of carbon quantum dots, such as lychee seeds, dragon fruit seeds, coconut husks, walnut shells, passion fruit shells, lemon peels, date seeds, and peanut shells, they have never been used in colorimetry [18].

Peanut shells have high fiber content, so they have recently been widely used as a carbon source to prepare cost-effective carbon quantum dots. Xue et al. prepared peanut shell CQD for the bioimaging application with a QY (Quantum yield) of 9.9 % [19]. Kun Huang and his fellow researchers have prepared the peanut shell CQD composites with KH-560 with 18.8 % QY and used them for the fluorometric detection of Fe3+ [20]. Lovepreet Singh enhanced the structural and functional properties of peanut shell CQDs with EDTA, and QY was 19.7 %, while no application was reported [21]. None of these researchers have neither functionalized the surface of CQDs nor used them for colorimetric application. We previously found that the QY of PSCA CQDs made from peanut shells and citric acid was enhanced by up to 21 %. Due to the complex composition of peanut shells, the synthesis procedures introduce many defects on the surface that reduce QY [18]. There is still a need to increase the quantum yield of peanut shell-derived CQDs and use them for colorimetric detection of heavy metals as this would be a safe, cost-effective, and rapid method.

Peanut shells and citric acid are collectively chosen as the carbon sources for synthesizing PSCA CQDs because of their low cost and high availability as well as the ability to synthesize CQDs with good properties. This material is also advantageous since peanut shells are natural, plentiful, and a renewable resource [22]. Peanut shells are composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, which makes them rich in carbon sources for the synthesis of CQDs [23]. These biopolymers, therefore, affect the structural characteristics and functional performances of the obtained CQDs. Since citric acid minimizes the surface complexity of peanut shells, non-radiative recombination centers form, and this enhances fluorescence quantum yield and emission efficiency of citric acid in the production of PSCA CQDs to produce high-quality products, citric acid is used in PSCA CQD synthesis [18]. The addition of sulfur powder and sodium sulfate appears to be useful for modifying the fluorescence and color-changing properties of peanut shell-derived CQDs (PSCAs) in a tunable manner. Sulfur effectively passivates the surface defects and reduces the non-radiative recombination which ultimately boosts the quantum yield of PSCA[24]. It also introduces the sulfur-containing functional groups. Sodium sulfate acts as a template influencing the size and morphology of the CQDs through the introduction of oxygen-containing groups on the surface. The combined effect of these new surface functionalities contributes to the significant increase in the quantum yield of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA as compared to unmodified PSCA CQDs [25]. In addition, it can benefit in tuning the emission wavelength for direct detection of heavy metals using colorimetry and improving fluorescence intensity to enhance sensitivity. Sulfur powder and sodium sulfate have never been used as modifiers for the surface functionalization of peanut shell CQDs. S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs appear to be promising alternatives to conventional colorimetric sensors for Pb²⁺ and Ag⁺ ions. Due to high sensitivity, selectivity, wide applicability, fast response, comparatively low cost, and eco-friendliness, they can be used in various applications. These CQDs can sensitively determine the low concentrations of heavy metals in various sample matrices and are highly stable, efficient, and reliable solutions for environmental monitoring. Utilization of peanut shells (abundant agricultural waste) as a carbon source, reduces the production cost of the CQDs which often rely on expensive precursors, and makes it suitable for the growing demand for sustainable and eco-friendly nanomaterials. The pyrolysis-based CQDs synthesis is inherently scalable which allows the large-scale production of the sensor while the modification of CQDs with the sulfur powder and sodium sulfate improves the quantum yield and selectivity of CQDs for specific metal ions. The colorimetric nature of the sensor enables the rapid and visual detection of heavy metals which makes it suitable for the on-site monitoring and point of care applications.

In this research, PSCA CQDs are modified with sulfur powder (S@PSCA) and sodium sulfate (SO4@PSCA) to use them for colorimetric sensing of heavy metals and to enhance their fluorescent properties for achieving high QYs. S-modified CQDs were used for the colorimetric sensing of heavy metal ions, and Pb2+ and Ag+ ions were selectively sensed. The colorimetric properties of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA were analyzed for the first time in this study. It was evaluated that S-addition has improved the fluorescent and metal sensing properties of PSCA by introducing different functionalities. Furthermore, real samples were also tested to check the efficiency of the sensors.

The peanut shells were bought from a nearby shop in Beijing, Chaoyang. The material was pyrolyzed in a Muffle furnace (KSL-1700X). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99.5 %), sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate, and sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate were utilized for the pH investigations, sulfur powder (sublimed), sodium sulfate, and citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) were utilized to modify peanut shell CQDs.

For the study of metal’s colorimetric detection: KCl, sodium acetate (CH3COONa), Pb (CH3COO)2.3H2O, magnesium sulfate anhydrous (MgSO4), anhydrous, cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (CoNO3)2.6H2O), lithium acetate dihydrate (CH3COOLi. 2H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2.2H2O), iron oxides (Fe3O4), copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu (NO3)2.3H2O), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4. 7H2O), and cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd (NO3)2·4H2O) were used. In the determination of quantum yield, quinine sulfate and sulfuric acid were used. Quantum dots were synthesized and purified through a dialysis membrane of 500 Da. The Merck company’s Milli-Q water system was used to obtain the ultra-pure water. All the chemicals were purchased from chemical companies and were used directly without going through a purification process.

Using a one-step pyrolysis procedure, CQDs created from peanut shells and citric acid (PSCA) in our previous work were modified with sodium sulfate and sulfur powder [18]. In the first step, peanut shells were washed and dried in the oven to remove all of the impurities and dust particles. In the second step, these dried peanut shells were ground and burned in a laboratory furnace to remove all the moisture of peanut shells. In the third step, 5 g of these burned peanut shells were mixed with 2 g of citric acid monohydrate (≥ 99 %) and 1 g of sulfur powder (≥ 99.98 %) in one crucible, while 5 g of these burned peanut shells were mixed with 2 g of citric acid monohydrate (≥ 99 %) and 1 g of sodium sulfate (≥ 99 %) in the second crucible. In the fourth step, these crucibles were placed in the muffle furnace and the reaction was carried out at 400 °C for 4 hours. Finally, the collected black powder of prepared sensors was mechanically grounded and sieved through 100 mesh size to collect uniform size particles. The powder was dissolved in water and was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter, and was dialyzed for 48 hours through a dialysis membrane for further analysis.

Two minutes after adding 1.5 ml of CQDs to 100 µM metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Zn+, Fe2+, Cd2+, K+, Ag+, Mg+, Ba2+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+), color changes were noticed. After that, a UV–vis examination was conducted on pure CQDs and CQDs containing metal ions. By using UV–vis analysis, the metal ions that changed color were confirmed, and the various concentrations were investigated. Prepared CQDs sensed the metal ion concentration in the range of 5–85 nM. The absorbance values of CQDs with varying metal ion concentrations were examined 5 times in the UV–vis spectrum and average values were taken to calculate the linearity (R2) and limit of detection. The sensing method for S@PSCA CQDs and SO4@PSCA CQDs was the same.

Tap water was collected from our laboratory (Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, China) and it was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 µm filter. Absorbance values of tap water were observed, then mixed with CQDs in a 1:1 ratio for heavy metal sensing. The potatoes and carrots were purchased from the nearest shop (Walmart, Beijing, China) and were washed with distilled water to avoid dust particles. Potatoes and carrots were crushed to collect their juices. They were centrifuged and filtered through 0.22 m filters to obtain their absorbance values. The juices were then mixed with CQDs for further analysis.

To examine the material's structural morphology, transmission electron microscopy was performed using a Hitachi-HT7700 (HITACHI, Japan) at a voltage of 120 KV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet is50, Japan) was used to measure the changes in the surface functional groups of PSCA CQDs following the addition of sulfur powder and sodium sulfate. An X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan) was used to assess the sample's crystal structure. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy system (THERMO ESCALAB 250, USA) was utilized for elemental analysis.

A Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer was used to record the excitation and emission of fluorescence in a room-temperature liquid solution. To measure decay time, the Edinburg instrument (FL-980) was employed. With UV2365 (Unico Shanghai Instrument Co., Ltd.), the absorbance values of all CQDs were recorded. Using the Quinine sulfate standard of the fixed quantum yield (Φf = 0.54 in 0.01 M H2SO4), a relative value was recorded. The QY was calculated using the following equation [18].
Q
sam
=
Q
ref
n

. The absorbance values of the sample and reference are

, respectively. The reference and sample fluorescence intensities are denoted by
I
ref

I
sam
, respectively. The refractive indices of the sample and the reference are represented by the values of
n

2

ref
2
.

In this research, previously reported peanut shell carbon quantum dots (PSCA-CQD) are modified with sulfur powder (S@PSCA) and sodium sulfate (SO4@PSCA)[18]. The addition of sulfur is added to PSCA to increase the quantum yield of the material and to utilize the peanut shells for colorimetric detection of heavy metals, which was not reported before. The structure and surface morphologies of the S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs were determined with the TEM analysis. The prepared S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs were spherical and uniformly distributed. The diameter of the particles was calculated through Image J software and 32 particles were selected for that. The mean value for S@PSCA CQDs was 5 and the standard deviation was 0.347, while the mean value for SO4@PSCA CQDs was 1.5, and the standard deviation was 0.347. The diameter of the particles ranged between 1 and 10 nm, with the average particle size between 4 and 6 nm ( Fig. 1A and 1B). The addition of sulfur compounds has caused minor changes to the particle size, and TEM results have shown that all of these CQDs are monodispersed.

The chemical composition of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs was investigated with the FTIR analysis and different characteristic peaks were obtained (Fig. 1C). For S@PSCA CQD, the peak at 3426 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of the -OH functional group. The peak at 1616 cm−1 represents the bending vibrations of H-O-H bonds, the peak appearing at 2858 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of C-H, and the peak at 1429 shows the presence of C-H bending vibrations in the S@PSCA CQD (Fig. 1C). The presence of two broad peaks at 1041 and 1149 cm−1 results from -SO3 2- and C-S bonds. Moreover, these S-modified CQDs produce an obvious signal of S-S linkage at 551 cm−1 [26]. For SO4@PSCA CQD, the peak at 3438 cm−1 was assigned to the OH group. Thus, the 1627 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 peaks in the SO4@PSCA CQDs’ FTIR spectrum correspond to the C-O and C-C functional groups. Two major peaks at 1150 cm−1, 989 cm−1, 455 cm−1 are associated with the –SO3 2-, symmetric stretch of -SO4 2-, and symmetric bend of -SO4 2- functional groups, respectively. The FTIR results have shown that PSCA CQDs were synthesized, and sulfur was successfully modified on the surface. The FTIR spectra of these CQDs have different characteristic peaks, and these variations of bonds have decreased the transmittance, which shows that the addition of sulfur powder and sodium sulfate has introduced new functionalities to PSCA CQD’s surface.

The XRD analysis has shown that the broad peak of PSCA at 2θ= 24.6° contains three sharp peaks appearing at 26°, 31°, and 45o, which correspond to the planes (004), (131), and (135) respectively (Fig. 1D), confirming the crystal structures and new interatomic spacing within the crystal lattice as a result of sulfur powder and sodium sulfate addition [27,28]. The change in the peak intensities and the broadening of original PSCA peaks has indicated that the addition of sulfur has changed the crystal structure, and sulfur functionalities are modified successfully on the surface.

The XPS analysis of S-modified CQDs was carried out to investigate the photoluminescence properties after modification. Our previous material of modified peanut shell CQDs (PSCA-CQD) had a peak of nitrogen at 400 eV, carbon at 284 eV, and oxygen at 533 eV with 19.4 % oxygen, 77 % carbon and 2.69 % Nitrogen[18]. The addition of sulfur powder has changed the atomic percentages of PSCA while forming S@PSCA CQD. It has a N1s peak at 400 eV, C1s peak at 284 eV, O1s peak at 532 eV, and S2p peak at 165 eV (Fig. S1). Along with these peaks, a sharp peak is also observed at 497 eV which is associated with the Na1s in the case of SO4@PSCA CQD and Auger peaks in the case of S@PSCA CQD. This peak is considered as impurity because it was not observed in the PSCA CQD, that’s why it is not quantified in the analysis. S@PSCA CQD has 55.6 % of C, 2.5 % of N, 35.19 % of O, and 6.5 % of S. C1s Scan has three peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.9 eV, and 285.8 eV which are related to C-C, C-N, and C-S bonds ( Fig. 2A). O1s scan has a peak at 31.2 eV for CO and 32.9 eV for S-O bond (Fig. 2B). N1s scan has three peaks at 399 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.2 eV for C-N-C, N-H, and N-C bonds (Fig. 2D). The S2p scan has 4 peaks. The peaks at 163.8 eV, 165.2 eV, 168.7 eV, and 169.8 eV are related to S2-(2p 1/2), S2-(2p 3/2), SO4 2- (2p 3/2), [29] and SO3 2- (Fig. 2C). The addition of sulfur powder to PSCA has reduced its Carbon percentage and increased the oxygen percentage due to the incorporation of sulfide (S2-), sulfite (SO3 2-), and sulfate (SO4 2-) functionalities. The peaks for π-π* transitions in the C1s scan of PSCA have also disappeared, which shows that PSCA CQD’s surface functionalities have been changed and PSCA has been successfully passivated with the sulfur powder. These functional groups bind with the surface defects of PSCA and fill the essential electron traps. It reduces the chances of excited electrons getting stuck in the electron traps. It enhances the probability of light emission which enhances the fluorescence properties of S@PSCA as compared to PSCA CQD.

The XPS spectra for the addition of sodium sulfate on PSCA give N1s peak at 400 eV, C1s peak at 284 eV, O1s peak at 532 eV, and S2p peak at 169 eV (Fig. S1). The SO4@PSCA has 48.98 % C, 2.8 % N, 43 % O, and 5 % S. The C1s scan has four peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 293.2 eV which are representing C-C, C-N, C-S bonds and π – π* transitions (Fig. 2E). O1s scan has three peaks at 531.5 eV, 532.7 eV, and 536 eV for CO, S-O, and H-O-H bonds (Fig. 2F). N1s Scan has three peaks at 399.1 eV, 400.5 eV, and 402.6 eV for C-N-C, N-H, and N-C bonds (Fig. 2H). S2p Scan has a peak at 170.2 for SO4 2- (2p 1/2), and 169.2 for SO4 2- (2p 3/2) (Fig. 2G). The addition of sodium sulfate has reduced the carbon percentage of PSCA and increased the percentage of oxygen and sulfur. It represents that sulfate functionalities have been changed and sodium sulfate is successfully modified on the surface of PSCA CQD. In the case of S@PSCA, the sulfide functionalities were higher than the sulfate and sulfite functionalities. That’s why the S2p peak appeared at 165 eV, which is a region of sulfides. In the case of SO4@PSCA, the sulfide functionalities are not present because sodium sulfate is highly stable, and the reaction condition of 400 °C is not good enough to break the sulfate ions into sulfides. Due to this reason, SO4@PSCA shows an S2p peak at 169 eV which is the region of sulfate ions. The presence of these sulfate ions creates an interaction with the surface charges of PSCA CQD leading towards a stable electrostatic environment. It reduces the chances of unwanted aggregation due to sulfides, facilitates the dispersion of SO4@PSCA CQD in the solution, and increases the QY of SO4@PSCA CQD higher than the S@PSCA CQD.

In our previous work, the optimum time and temperature to achieve highly fluorescent PSCA-CQD was 400 °C for 4 hours because peanut shells are completely carbonized at this time and temperature [18]. Any change in these optimum conditions will affect the fluorescent properties of the PSCA CQD. That’s why these conditions are kept constant in this research, and the concentration of sulfur powder and sodium sulfate is optimized to achieve the high quantum yield of the S-modified PSCA CQDs. The sublimed sulfur powder has a large surface area with a small particle size, which increases its reactivity with the peanut shells and citric acid. With a pyrolysis temperature and time of 400 °C for 4 hours, the sulfur powder can interact with peanut shells and citric acid to the maximum. Still, a further increase in temperature or time may cause an undesirable reaction since peanut shells will completely carbonize and turn into ashes, which are incapable of producing fluorescent carbon quantum dots [22]. The concentration of the sulfur powder was optimized between 0.5 g to 4 g and it was found that 1 g concentration is optimum for achieving the high QY of the S@PSCA CQD ( Fig. 3B).

This concentration of 1 g is sufficient to passivate the defective sites of PSCA, which trap the excited electrons and reduce their fluorescent ability. The filling of these trap sites through sulfur enhances the overall fluorescent properties of S@PSCA CQD. If the concentration is less than 1 g, then the quantity would not be sufficient to cover these trap sites. If the concentration is higher than 1 g then it will cause excessive passivation and will decrease the fluorescence properties of S@PSCA. The calculated quantum yield of S@PSCA CQD is 27.3 %, which is higher than the reported QY of PSCA CQD. The maximum excitation peak of S@PSCA CQD is observed at 320 nm, and the emission peak at 414 nm (Fig. 3A). The fluorescence intensity of the S@PSCA CQD was also observed in the excitation range of 280–400 nm (Fig. 3C). It was found that increasing the excitation energy decreased the fluorescence intensity, and the peak is red-shifted from 414 to 467 nm due to a change in surface functionalities of the PSCA CQD and the formation of new electronic states in the band gaps [30].

Sodium sulfate is an inorganic salt, also known as salt cake. It has good thermal stability, and it does not decompose at the pyrolysis condition of 400 °C for 4 hrs. with PSCA. It plays a supporting role in enhancing the fluorescent properties of the PSCA CQD and its concentration was optimized between 0.5 g to 1 g (Fig. 3E). The optimum concentration of sodium sulfate that is required to enhance the fluorescent properties of PSCA is 1 g. During pyrolysis, sodium sulfate helps to remove the excessive water of the PSCA and improve the porosity and final morphology of SO4@PSCA CQD. The sodium sulfate salt dissociates into sulfate (SO4 2-) and sodium (Na+) ions. Being negatively charged, these sulfate ions interact with the surface charges of PSCA and create a stable electrostatic environment, which reduces the aggregation and improves the dispersibility, leading to enhanced fluorescence of SO4@PSCA CQD in comparison to PSCA CQD.

If the concentration of the sodium sulfate decreases or increases from 1 g, then a favorable electrostatic environment will deviate and will decrease the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3E). The calculated QY of the SO4@PSCA CQD is 36.4 %. The maximum excitation peak of SO4@PSCA CQD is observed at 321 nm, and the emission peak at 415 nm (Fig. 3D). The shift in the emission wavelength was also observed by changing the excitation wavelength from 280 to 400 nm, and the red shift was observed from 415 to 476 nm with the decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3F). This redshift is observed due to the reduced aggregation of SO4@PSCA CQD as a result of sodium sulfate addition, as the primary benefit of this addition is to prevent the aggregation-induced quenching of the PSCA CQD.

To observe the absorption peaks of modified PSCA CQDs, they were dissolved in water forming colorless solutions showing that they are highly soluble in water. The UV spectrum of S@PSCA CQD has a broad peak at 280 nm, and SO4@PSCA CQD has a peak at 278 nm ( Fig. 4a). These peaks are associated with the n-π* transitions [31]. The π-π* transitions of PSCA have disappeared in S@PSCA CQD due to changes in the electronic structures as a result of Sulfur addition. That’s why n-π* transitions have a visually broad peak in the UV spectrum. While SO4@PSCA also has a peak of n-π* transitions in the UV spectra, it also retains the π-π* transitions of PSCA, as shown in the XPS scan of C1s scans (Fig. 2E). The reason is that sodium sulfate of SO4@PSCA does not directly involve the conjugated π-electron system of PSCA and influences the dispersion and aggregation of CQD. While sulfur is directly involved with the conjugated π-electron system of PSCA, that’s why it does not show peaks for π-π* transitions.

The photoluminescence study has also revealed that the decay time of S@PSCA is 9.7 ns and SO4@PSCA is 7.8 ns, which is greater than the reported 4.98 ns decay time [18] of PSCA CQD (Fig. 4B). The addition of sulfur introduces some trap states in the PSCA structure where electrons spend a longer time, leading towards higher decay time[32]. The pH stability of all the CQDs was observed in a fluorescence spectrophotometer and UV–visible instrument in a pH range of 1–12 (Fig. 4C–4F). It was found that S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs have stable fluorescence and absorbance in all pH ranges, which makes them attractive options for future applications such as light-emitting diodes and fluorescence imaging [33]. All of these CQDs have better quantum yields than previously reported materials, as shown in Table 1.

The sensing mechanism of S@PSCA CQDs and SO₄ @ PSCA CQDs depends on the change in color and absorbance peak intensities in the UV–visible spectrophotometer. In the case of S@PSCA, the interaction of Pb²⁺ or Ag⁺ ions with the sulfide groups in PSCA induces surface energy changes and electrostatic interactions leading towards the partial aggregation of the CQDs rather than the formation of distinct metal sulfide complexes. This aggregation changes the dispersion state of the CQDs in the solution, causing the change in optical properties and ultimately decreasing the absorbance intensity without the appearance of new absorption band. Therefore, decrease in the intensity of absorption band centered at 275 nm can be attributed to the decrease in availability of the free CQDs due to the agglomeration, rather than the complexation which can be seen through the color change ( Fig. 5A and 5B). These properties contribute to the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor for the detection of Pb²⁺ and Ag⁺ ions as the agglomeration effect provide the visual mean of detecting these metal ions [38]. This makes the S@PSCA CQDs a useful tool for many applications, such as environmental monitoring systems, food quality analysis, and industrial applications.

For SO₄@PSCA CQDs, the interaction of Ag⁺ with SO₄²⁻ on the CQD surface leads to the formation of Ag₂SO₄, a low-solubility salt that induces turbidity in the solution. This process results in the precipitation of CQDs, reducing the number of freely dispersed particles and consequently decreasing absorbance intensity. The observed turbidity serve as effective indicators for Ag⁺ detection [39]. These changes in the solution can be used as an obvious and basic way for the identification of Ag⁺ ions. The sensing mechanism is primarily based on aggregation and precipitation effects rather than the formation of new chemical complexes with distinct absorption bands. The formation of Ag₂SO₄ complexes has several benefits, such as high sensitivity, specificity, and easy detection, making SO₄@PSCA CQDs applicable to multiple uses. The reproducibility and detection stability of the sensor was examined by repeating the experiment 5 times. The sensor’s detection ability was 99–100 % in every experiment which shows that the sensor is reproducible with good performance and makes it suitable to be applied for commercialization (Fig. S6).

The synthesized CQDs were tested as a colorimetric sensor for the determination of heavy metals. Different responses were obtained when S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs reacted with the 100 µM concentrations of heavy metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Zn+, Fe2+, Cd2+, K+, Ag+, Mg+, Ba2+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+) and their interaction was recorded through the UV-Visible spectrum. In our previous work of enhancing the fluorescent properties of PSCA-CQDs, it was observed that PSCA was suitable for fluorometric detection of Cu2+ ions, and no visual change of color was observed with all heavy metals. S@PSCA CQD has reacted with Pb2+ and Ag+ among all the metals, and the changes were observed with a UV–visible spectrophotometer and visually (Fig. 5A). S@PSCA has shifted absorbance values at 275 nm and 271 nm values of Pb2+ and Ag+ in comparison to other metal ions, and it gives precipitates with the Pb2+ and yellow color with Ag+ (Fig. 5A and 5B). The reason is that sulfide (S2-) functionalities of S@PSCA react to form the PbS@PSCA and Ag2S@PSCA complexes. The obtained results have demonstrated that S@PSCA CQD has the potential to be used as a colorimetric sensor for Pb2+ and Ag+.

Once it was determined that S@PSCA CQD has a selective response for Pb2+ and Ag+, their absorbance was examined with a UV–visible spectrophotometer at different concentrations ranging from 5 to 85 nM. It was observed that, by increasing the concentration of metal ions, the absorbance was linearly decreased for both Pb2+ and Ag+ ions due to the complexation on the surface of the CQDs ( Fig. 6A and 6C). The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor was determined from the equation: LOD = 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation value from the blank study and “m” is the slope value, which is obtained through the calibration curve of absorbance versus Pb2+ and Ag+ ion concentrations. The slope was determined for the absorbance values of the concentration starting from 5 to 85 nM. The calculated LOD of the S@PSCA sensor for Pb2+ and Ag+ was found to be 3.8 nM and 9 nM. The limit of quantification (LOQ) is also significant in the colorimetric study for the detection of metal ions. The LOQ was determined using the formula LOQ = 10σ/m, and it was found to be 11.6 and 27.3 nM for Pb2+ and Ag+. Furthermore, we also compared our results with the colorimetric study that was already published for Pb2+ and Ag+ in the literature, as shown in Table 2.

The interference study of Pb2+ and Ag+ with S@PSCA CQD has shown that selective metals are not interfered with by other metals, and the addition of Ag+ does not affect the absorbance of Pb2+. However, the addition of Pb2+ reduces the absorbance of Ag+ when added as an interference (Fig. S2 and S3). The reason is that Pb2+ has a higher affinity for the sulfide (S2-) functionalities of the S@PSCA CQD as compared to Ag+ and it competes against Ag+ for the binding sites. That’s when Ag+ is added as an interference with the Pb2+. Most of the binding sites are already occupied, and this pre-occupation leads to very limited interaction with Ag+. When Pb2+ is added as interference, it competes with Ag+ for bindi ng sites, and as a result of this competition, a stearic hindrance is caused, and the absorbance of Ag+ decreases. In the water quality standards, the concentration of Pb2+ in tap water is 5 µg/L, and Ag+ is 0.46 µmol/L. At the same time, the detection of S@PSCA CQD is lower than this concentration, which makes it a promising probe for colorimetric detection [40].

SO4@PSCA CQD has reacted with Ag+ and gives a milky white color visually, and in the UV spectra, its absorbance is recorded at 258 nm as a result of complexation with Ag+ ions while the other metals that do not react (Fig. 5b). The reason is that when sulfate ion (SO4 2-) of SO4@PSCA interact with the Ag+ ions, they form a silver sulfate (Ag2SO4@PSCA) complex, which is poorly soluble in water and gives a milky white color. In order to confirm the selectivity of the SO4@PSCA for Ag+, 100 µM concentrations of heavy metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Zn+, Fe2+, Cd2+, K+, Mg+, Ba2+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+) was added and UV responses were examined. It was found that other metals do not interfere with the interaction of SO4@PSCA with Ag+, which makes it a suitable sensor for colorimetric detection (Fig. S4 and S5). Once the selectivity was confirmed, the SO4@PSCA sensor was checked for different concentrations of Ag+ ranging from 5 to 85 nM through a UV–visible spectrophotometer to calculate the LOD (Fig. 6E). It was found that there is a linear relationship between the UV absorbance and the concentration of Ag+, which is why increasing the metal concentration has increased the UV absorbance. The UV absorption of the Ag2SO4 complex interferes and contributes to the S@PSCA CQDs' absorption which ultimately increases with the concentration of Ag+ ions (Fig. 6F). SO4@PSCA has a limit of detection of 8.2 nM, which is lower than the concentration of Ag+ in water determined by the World Health Organization [26]. The calculated LOQ is 25.1 nM. The LODs for the developed sensors are well below EPA-MCLs for drinking water. The MCL for Pb2+ is 15 µg/L (0.072 µM) and the MCL for Ag+ is 10 µg/L (0.093 µM) [41]. According to the interference analysis of these sensors, in the case of S@PSCA, Ag+ has an absorbance wavelength of 272 nm and Pb2+ has an absorbance wavelength of 278 nm. The absorbance wavelengths for both of these metals are not affected by any of the interferences due to their high affinity for the sulfide (S2-) functionalities while the absorbance of Ag+ slightly decreases which shows the presence of Pb2+ ions in the solution (Fig. S3). In the case of SO4@PSCA CQDs the absorbance wavelengths and intensities are not affected by any other interferences, this implies that both sensors are capable of distinguishing the Pb2+ and Ag+ ions at concentrations that are realistic in the real world.

The effectiveness of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA for heavy metal sensing was evaluated through real sample testing. To detect heavy metal pollution in real samples, a known (0.5 µM) concentration of metal ions for each sample was added to tap water, potato juice, and carrot juice, and colorimetric tests were performed in triplicate. The obtained results are shown in Table 3, which demonstrates the potential of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs to be used as colorimetric sensors for metal ions in real samples. The implementation of this synthesis methodology at an industrial scale may face some obstacles. To achieve scalable synthesis, three essential elements must be present: reproducible results by maintaining batch-to-batch consistency, perfect material properties, and cost-efficient and environmentally friendly production design. Main challenges regarding scalability can be tackled through reaction optimizations and continuous flow synthesis techniques in addition to using green chemistry methods. Successful scale-up of large-scale manufacturing requires efficient techniques for both product separation and purification. To close the production gap between lab experiments and real-world industrial usage, future research should focus on developing dependable and expandable synthesis techniques.

PSCA CQDs modified with sulfur compounds were synthesized and characterized, and their colorimetric sensing activity for heavy metals in real samples was investigated. The peaks of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA CQDs in FTIR were observed and it was found that PSCA was modified successfully. The effect of addition on the size and shape of the PSCA particles was analyzed with TEM analysis and no significant change was observed in the shape and size of the particles. In addition, the change in optical properties of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA was observed with XPS, fluorescence spectrophotometry, and UV–visible. The results indicated that UV–visible spectra show a shift in peaks of PSCA and QY was also comparatively higher. The addition of sulfur was found to be very effective in changing the optical properties of the PSCA. The sensor properties of S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA, CQDs were investigated, and it was found that S@PSCA enabled the colorimetric detection of Pb2+ and Ag+, and LOD was calculated as 3.83 nM and 9 nM. In contrast, SO4@PSCA enabled the colorimetric detection of Ag+ ion and the LOD is 8.2 nM. The environmental protection authority has mentioned MCL for Pb2+ 15 µg/L (0.072 µM) and the MCL for Ag+ is 10 µg/L (0.093 µM) which is higher than the LOD of these sensors making them suitable for environmental testing. The addition of sulfur and sodium sulfate to PSCA enhances its fluorescent ability with the colorimetric detection property to detect heavy metals in food and water samples. S@PSCA has good colorimetric selectivity for Pb²⁺ and Ag⁺ ions with low detection limits in comparison to SO4@PSCA, while SO4@PSCA has an excellent ability to enhance the overall fluorescence of the PSCA. By combining these two effects, S@PSCA and SO4@PSCA materials perform better than the original PSCA material, resulting in enhanced sensitivity and selectivity for heavy metals. Therefore, compared to PSCA, these S-modified CQDs have a wide application prospect in environmental monitoring and food safety.

Du Zhenxia: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chen Shu-Huan: Formal analysis. Keshat Basem E.: Writing – review & editing. Abbas Muhammad Qamer: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Javeria Huma: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.115821.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sener, G., L. Uzun, and A. Denizli, Colorimetric sensor array based on gold nanoparticles and amino acids for identification of toxic metal ions in water. ACS applied materials & interfaces, 2014. 6(21): p. 18395-18400.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective colorimetric detection of Pb (II) ions by using green synthesized gold nanoparticles with orange peel extract
Chemosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zannotti, M., et al., Selective colorimetric detection of Pb (II) ions by using green synthesized gold nanoparticles with orange peel extract. Chemosensors, 2024. 12(3): p. 33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cayuela, A., et al., Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions. Talanta, 2016. 151: p. 100-105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral CQD-based PL and CD sensors for high sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fan, X., et al., Chiral CQD-based PL and CD sensors for high sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions. Optical Materials, 2023. 137: p. 113620.
